content.claim: Hungary is an OECD outlier on socioeconomic educational inequity: children's economic, social, and cultural status (ESCS) explains over 20% of variance in reading, mathematics, and science achievement (exceeding the 12–16% OECD average); disadvantaged students have less than a 1-in-8 chance of attending the same school as high achievers; performance varies by more than three years of schooling between top and bottom SES quintiles.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: PISA measures 15-year-olds already sorted into tracks, cannot isolate causal contribution of 6/8-year gimnázium specifically versus broader 3-way secondary tracking
Cross-national comparison does not identify mechanism responsible for Hungarian outlier status
School-type variation by SES is multiply-documented but attribution to tracked structure is inferential rather than causal
Data do not separate effect of gimnázium early selection from residential/ethnic segregation or other structural factors
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
